findings.title: CONVERGENCE_SCHEMA allows evaluation without findings → false 'converged'
findings.description: CONVERGENCE_SCHEMA declares `findings` as optional (`required: []`). When someone calls pipeline_convergence without findings AND the loaded state also has empty findings, evaluate_convergence() returns decision='converged' — semantically incorrect: no evaluation was actually performed. The schema description says 'If omitted — reads from saved state' but the saved state may be empty/fresh.
findings.impact: Calling pipeline_convergence on a fresh pipeline with no findings returns 'converged' — the pipeline appears done before any work. This is a logic error: absence of findings ≠ convergence.
findings.reproduction: 1. pipeline_save({request:'test', status:'running', ...}) with no findings. 2. Call pipeline_convergence() without findings arg. 3. Returns: {decision:'converged', reason:'No P0/P1 findings (0 P2 advisories)'}
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Make findings REQUIRED in CONVERGENCE_SCHEMA, OR 2. Add a guard in handle_convergence: if findings is None and not state.get('findings'): return {decision:'unknown', reason:'No findings available — cannot evaluate convergence'}. Option 2 is safer — doesn't break callers that omit findings on purpose.

findings.title: No defense-in-depth validation of findings in handler layer
findings.description: While JSON Schema validates the tool parameter schema (severity enum, required fields), the handler and evaluate_convergence rely on `.get()` defaults without validating beyond the schema boundary. Specifically: (a) handle_convergence does not validate that each finding dict has a non-empty severity string — it trusts the LLM agent to pass valid values; (b) pstate.load() reads raw JSON from disk with NO schema validation at all — stale/corrupted state.json bypasses all validation; (c) _compute_fingerprint uses `.get('description', '')[:80]` which silently accepts missing descriptions.
findings.impact: A corrupted state.json or an LLM hallucination producing findings with missing/invalid severity could silently produce wrong convergence decisions.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add a `_validate_findings(findings: list) -> list` function that: (1) filters out dicts with missing required keys, (2) normalizes severity to uppercase, (3) warns but doesn't crash on invalid entries. Call it in handle_convergence before saving findings and in pstate.load() before returning state.

findings.title: Dead code in evaluate_convergence: unreachable second 'converged' check
findings.description: Lines 134-144 contain a duplicate converged check that is NEVER reachable: (a) if p0p1_findings was empty, the function already returned at lines 112-120; (b) if round >= max_rounds, the function already returned at lines 123-132. The second check at lines 134-144 is dead code — introduces confusion and a maintenance hazard.
findings.impact: Code smell, maintenance hazard. No runtime impact (dead code).
findings.fix_suggestion: Delete lines 134-144 and the `p0p1_findings = list(p0) + list(p1)` on line 109 (it's computed twice — lines 109 and 135). Remove the duplicate entirely.

findings.title: No file locking on state.json — lost-update race condition under concurrency
findings.description: save() uses atomic rename (os.replace) — correct for preventing partial writes — but does NOT use file locking (flock/fcntl). Two concurrent pstate.load() + pstate.save() cycles can lose updates: Process A loads, Process B loads, Process A modifies + saves, Process B modifies + saves (overwriting A). The TTL check on load() doesn't prevent this — it only handles expiry.
findings.impact: P2 severity because Hermes runs single-threaded per session. This only matters if: (a) two Hermes sessions run the same pipeline concurrently (unlikely), or (b) the plugin is ever used with multi-threaded tool dispatch.
findings.fix_suggestion: Option A: Use `fcntl.flock()` with LOCK_EX on the state file for the duration of load+modify+save. Option B (simpler for single-session use): add a `_generation` counter to the state file that save() checks (optimistic locking) — load reads gen, save writes gen+1, if gen changed since load → raise StaleStateError. Option B is lightweight and sufficient.

findings.title: ARCHITECTURE.md state schema is stale — missing convergence fields
findings.description: ARCHITECTURE.md documents the state schema without the convergence-loop fields (round, max_rounds, findings, findings_fingerprint, prev_findings_fingerprint, convergence). These fields are present in the actual state.py code and state.json but absent from the architecture docs.
findings.impact: Documentation drift — new developers reading ARCHITECTURE.md won't understand the convergence loop state.
findings.fix_suggestion: Update ARCHITECTURE.md state schema section to include convergence fields. Add a note that the convergence evaluator is deterministic and lives in state.py.

findings.title: User-claimed bugs that are NOT present in current code
findings.description: Three claims from the user's pre-audit hypothesis are not reproducible in the current codebase: (1) 'save() mutates passed dict in-place' — FIXED. save() does `snapshot = dict(state)` (shallow copy) before any mutations, and only uses setdefault() on the snapshot copy. (2) 'double-load in handle_convergence' — NOT PRESENT. There's exactly one pstate.load() at line 183, then evaluate_convergence uses the in-memory state. No second load. (3) 'no atomic write' — INCORRECT. save() uses os.replace(tmp_path, STATE_PATH) which is atomic on POSIX (guaranteed rename, no partial file).
findings.impact: These issues appear to have been fixed in a recent commit (post-refactoring). The code audit confirms they are resolved.
findings.fix_suggestion: No action needed — confirm with git log when these fixes were applied. The audit confirms the fixes are correct.

responsibility_separation.state_vs_init: GOOD. state.py handles pure state persistence (save/load/clear) + deterministic convergence evaluation. __init__.py handles Hermes tool registration, JSON Schema definitions, and handler wiring. Clear separation — state.py has zero knowledge of Hermes; __init__.py has zero business logic.
responsibility_separation.evaluate_convergence_placement: CORRECT. evaluate_convergence lives in state.py alongside the state it evaluates. It's deterministic (no LLM) — consistent with the design principle. Could be split into a separate convergence.py if it grows beyond ~30 lines, but for now it's appropriately placed.
responsibility_separation.classify_placement: CORRECT. classify.py is independent — pure keyword matching, no state dependency. Clean module.

patterns.handler_pattern: CORRECT. Each handler is a pure function taking (args, **kwargs) → JSON string. No side effects except via pstate. Clear try/except wrapping. Good error reporting with traceback.
patterns.schema_pattern: CORRECT. JSON Schema definitions in module-level constants. Hermes-native validation before handler invocation. Schema and handler colocated.
patterns.concurrency_pattern: ACCEPTABLE for current use. Single-session Hermes + serial pipeline execution means locking is unnecessary today. If this ever runs with parallel tool dispatch, AUDIT-004 becomes P1.
patterns.error_handling_pattern: GOOD. All handlers have try/except. load() catches JSONDecodeError. save() does cleanup on failure (removes tmp file). TTL expiry guards against stale state.

what_could_be_improved: evaluate_convergence should be extracted to a separate convergence.py module if it grows (currently ~85 lines — borderline)
Add a `PipelineState` dataclass/typeddict instead of passing raw dicts — would catch missing keys at development time
Consider making MODEL_MAP configurable via plugin.yaml or a separate config file instead of hardcoded
Add integration tests that exercise the full load→modify→save→evaluate cycle (currently tested in isolation)

recommendations.action: Fix AUDIT-001: Guard against false 'converged' when no findings available
recommendations.implementation: In handle_convergence, before calling evaluate_convergence, check: if findings is None and not state.get('findings'): return {decision:'unknown', reason:'No findings — cannot evaluate convergence'}.

recommendations.action: Fix AUDIT-002: Add findings validation in handler layer
recommendations.implementation: Add `_validate_findings()` in state.py: filter out malformed entries, normalize severity case, log warnings. Call in handle_convergence before save and in pstate.load() for loaded state.

recommendations.action: Fix AUDIT-003: Remove dead code in evaluate_convergence
recommendations.implementation: Delete lines 134-144 in state.py. Remove duplicate `p0p1_findings = list(p0) + list(p1)` on line 109 (keep only line 109 version since it's used in fingerprint computation on line 147).

recommendations.action: Fix AUDIT-004: Add optimistic locking to state.json
recommendations.implementation: Add `_generation` integer to state dict. save() checks that the generation on disk matches the generation loaded. If mismatch → raise StaleStateError. Increment generation on successful save.

recommendations.action: Fix AUDIT-005: Update ARCHITECTURE.md state schema
recommendations.implementation: Update the state schema section to include convergence fields (round, max_rounds, findings, findings_fingerprint, prev_findings_fingerprint, convergence).
If evaluate_convergence grows beyond ~50 lines, extract to convergence.py. Move _compute_fingerprint with it. Keep state.py for pure persistence (save/load/clear). Currently at 85 lines — borderline.

recommendations.action: Document the audit findings in significant-changes-summary.mdc
recommendations.implementation: Note that three user-claimed bugs were found to be already fixed (save side-effect, double-load, missing atomic write). Document the real issues found (AUDIT-001 through AUDIT-005).

verdict_rationale: Two P1 issues (false 'converged' on empty findings, missing findings validation) require changes before the convergence loop can be trusted in production. The P2 issues are code-quality concerns that should be addressed but don't block operation. The three user-claimed critical issues (save() side-effect, double-load, no atomic write) are NOT present in the current code — they appear to have been fixed in a recent refactoring. The architecture fundamentals (separation of concerns, deterministic control flow, handler patterns) are solid.
